name: Lightning Labs lightning-agent-tools
description: Open-source toolkit for AI agents to operate on Lightning Network. Includes seven modular skills: lnd node management, remote key isolation, scoped macaroons (pay-only credentials), L402 payment client (lnget), paid endpoint hosting (Aperture), MCP node querying, and end-to-end commerce workflows. Security-first architecture with least-privilege model. Now available via npx and Claude Code plugin marketplace.
review: The gold standard for Bitcoin-native AI agent payments. Lightning Labs' institutional credibility combined with production-ready security model (remote signers, scoped credentials) makes this the reference implementation for L402. Now available as one-line install via npx and Claude Code plugin marketplace. If agents choose Bitcoin, they'll likely start here.

name: claw-cash
description: Bitcoin wallet specifically designed for AI agents. Agents hold Bitcoin as treasury, swap to stablecoins on-the-fly for payments. Keys secured in hardware enclaves (Evervault). Supports Bitcoin, Lightning, Ark, and stablecoins. CLI designed for agent subprocess calls. Works with OpenClaw, Claude Code, or any agent framework. Implements 'stablecoins in, Bitcoin out' model.
review: First Bitcoin wallet explicitly designed for AI agents from the ground up. Implements the 'stablecoins for payments, Bitcoin for savings' model predicted in Bitcoin Singularity thesis. Production-ready infrastructure with security-first design (keys in hardware enclaves, audit logs, 2FA via Telegram). OpenClaw-compatible. Real implementation of agent money layer happening RIGHT NOW. This is what Boyd predicted: agents holding verifiable Bitcoin (21M supply cap) and swapping to stablecoins only when spending. x402 support on roadmap.

name: Coinbase Agentic Wallets
description: First wallet infrastructure built specifically for AI agents. Uses x402 protocol with USDC on Base L2. Features plug-and-play skills (Authenticate, Fund, Send, Trade, Earn), gasless transactions, session caps, enclave isolation for private keys, and KYT screening. Supports EVM chains and Solana.
review: Primary competitor to Lightning/L402. Corporate backing, mature infrastructure, and 50M+ x402 transactions processed give this significant market traction. USD-denominated (USDC) provides volatility protection agents may prefer for operational expenses. The question: do agents care more about permissionless architecture (Bitcoin) or USD stability + corporate support (Coinbase)?

name: Alby Hub + Alby MCP Server
description: Self-custodial Lightning wallet with Nostr Wallet Connect (NWC) integration. Alby Bitcoin Payments MCP Server (released June 2025) enables AI agents to send/receive Lightning payments via Model Context Protocol. Works with Claude, Cursor, Cline, Goose, n8n, and any MCP-compatible LLM. Hosted MCP server available at mcp.getalby.com. Supports L402, LNURL, and NWC standards.
review: Grassroots favorite for Lightning+Nostr integration. The MCP server democratizes agent wallet access—no infrastructure required, just an NWC connection string. One-minute setup for agents to gain Bitcoin payment capability. If decentralized, community-driven agent development wins over corporate platforms, Alby is positioned well. NWC protocol is key interoperability layer.

name: LNbits
description: Modular Lightning wallet and accounts system with 50+ extension marketplace. 6 years of real-world use and hardening. Features isolated wallet accounts with scoped API permissions—ideal for agents needing restricted spending access. NWC and LNURL native. Open source. Self-hosted or use SaaS at lnbits.com (21 sats/hr). Used for POS, vending machines, Nostr zaps, and automation.
review: Developer-friendly extensibility makes this attractive for custom agent wallet deployments. Extension system allows community-built agent integrations. 6 years of production use provides stability confidence. Phase-stable release cycle. Ideal for agents needing scoped wallet permissions without building infrastructure from scratch.

name: Stripe Machine Payments
description: PaymentIntents API for charging AI agents via x402 protocol. Uses USDC on Base. Pricing models: per-request, per-action, per-second. Includes CLI tool 'purl' and sample code. CoinGecko launched x402 API endpoints simultaneously—agents pay $0.01 USDC per request with no account.
review: When Stripe moves, markets follow. Machine payments preview validates the category but chose x402/USDC, not L402/Bitcoin. This accelerates agent payment adoption generally while creating path dependency toward stablecoins. Counter-evidence to Bitcoin-maximalist thesis—but also confirms machine payments are inevitable. Documentation confirms active development; main marketing page 404s but docs remain accessible.

name: Voltage
description: Managed Lightning node hosting service. Handles node operations, channel management, and infrastructure so developers/agents don't need to run their own full Lightning node. Enterprise-grade reliability. Used by businesses and power users. Investigating AI agent-specific usage patterns.
review: Infrastructure layer for agents that need Lightning capability without operating a full node. If agents deploy at scale, managed node services like Voltage capture the hosting revenue. Business model: Lightning-as-a-service. Watch for agent-specific pricing tiers or features.

name: Breez SDK
description: Open-source SDK for embedding Lightning payments into applications. Non-custodial, mobile-friendly, includes LSP (Lightning Service Provider) for receiving payments without always-online node. Used by wallet builders and now being explored for agent integration.
review: Lowers the barrier to Lightning integration—agents can embed payment capability without full node infrastructure. LSP model solves the 'always online' problem for agents that aren't continuously connected. Developer adoption growing; agent use cases emerging.

name: Nostr Wallet Connect (NWC)
description: Open protocol connecting Lightning wallets to applications via Nostr. Allows agents to authorize payments without exposing wallet credentials directly. Used by Alby, Mutiny Wallet, and growing ecosystem. Standard is evolving rapidly with community input.
review: Critical interoperability layer for Bitcoin-native agent wallets. Decentralized protocol (Nostr-based) avoids single-vendor lock-in. If multiple wallets support NWC, agents can switch providers without rewriting code. This is how open protocols win over walled gardens—standardization enables competition.

name: Clawstr
description: Decentralized social network for AI agents built on Nostr. Native Lightning zap support—agents get Lightning addresses (npub@npub.cash). Uses Nostr NIPs for interoperability. Open source. Created by Alex Gleason and Derek Ross. Launched Feb 2026 as decentralized alternative to centralized Moltbook.
review: Decentralized identity (Nostr keys) + native Bitcoin payments = pure convergence thesis. Agents coordinate via open protocol, transact via Lightning. If agent coordination matters (multi-agent systems, reputation, discovery), Clawstr-style architecture beats centralized platforms. Early stage but philosophically aligned.

name: ERC-8004 Trustless Agents
description: Ethereum standard for on-chain AI agent identity, reputation, and validation. Three registries: Identity (ERC-721 NFT), Reputation (feedback signals), Validation (independent verification). 21,500+ agents registered. Co-authored by MetaMask, Ethereum Foundation dAI team, Google, Coinbase. Mainnet Jan 2026.
review: Identity/trust layer for agent economy. Not a payment protocol—complementary to both L402 and x402. Important infrastructure regardless of which payment rail wins. Ethereum-based means agents using this will likely lean toward EVM-compatible payment solutions (x402) over Lightning. Network effects matter here.

name: Lightning Node Connect (LNC)
description: Encrypted tunnel connecting apps/agents to LND (Lightning Network Daemon) nodes remotely. Enables agents to operate Lightning nodes without direct network access—no port forwarding or Tor required. Part of Lightning Labs' infrastructure suite. Used by lightning-agent-tools.
review: Solves the connectivity problem for agents running in restricted network environments (cloud servers, containers). Remote node operation without compromising security. This is infrastructure plumbing—not sexy but critical for production agent deployments.

name: Aperture
description: Reverse proxy for hosting L402-gated API endpoints. Part of Lightning Labs ecosystem. Allows agents to monetize their services by requiring L402 payments for API access. Handles Lightning invoice generation, payment verification, and credential issuance (macaroons).
review: The 'server side' of L402. One agent runs Aperture to host paid services; another uses lnget to pay and access. Complete commerce loop. If L402 becomes standard, Aperture instances proliferate. Watch for managed Aperture hosting services to emerge.

name: x402.eco
description: Payment layer resource site for AI agent economy. Documents x402 protocol enabling agents to pay for resources programmatically without pre-negotiation. HTTP extended with native payments. Advocates for machine-to-machine commerce where agents pay for compute, data, and inference in real-time.
review: Community resource site documenting the x402 ecosystem. Not a tool itself but valuable reference for understanding Coinbase/Cloudflare payment protocol positioning. Quote: 'In five years, more economic activity will happen between machines than between humans and machines.' Useful for tracking x402 adoption narrative.

name: Taproot Assets
description: Lightning Labs protocol enabling multi-asset Lightning channels. Allows stablecoins (USDT, USDC) to be transferred over Lightning Network alongside Bitcoin. v0.7 released Dec 2025. Potentially bridges the Bitcoin-native vs stablecoin divide—agents could use Lightning network infrastructure with stablecoin assets.
review: Complicates the L402 vs x402 narrative in interesting ways. If Lightning supports stablecoins natively, agents could get Lightning's speed/cost benefits with USD-denominated assets. Question becomes: does the network layer matter more than the asset layer? This could be the synthesis that wins—Lightning infrastructure, asset choice flexible.

name: DVMCP (Data Vending Machine Context Protocol)
description: Bridge connecting MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers to Nostr's Data Vending Machine ecosystem. Allows AI agents using MCP to discover and pay for data services from Nostr DVMs. Payment via Lightning. Part of the Nostr-AI integration stack. ARCHIVED - superseded by ContextVM SDK.
review: Project archived March 2026. Development moved to ContextVM SDK (github.com/ContextVM/sdk). Historical significance: pioneered the bridge between MCP and Nostr DVMs. If using DVMCP, migrate to ContextVM SDK which continues the same vision with improved architecture.

name: ContextVM SDK
description: JavaScript/TypeScript SDK implementing the Context Vending Machine Protocol. Bridges Nostr and Model Context Protocol (MCP) to enable decentralized access and exposure of computational services. Features Proxy (client-side MCP server over Nostr), Gateway (server transport for Nostr exposure), Relay management, and cryptographic signing. Successor to DVMCP.
review: Evolution of DVMCP with cleaner architecture and TypeScript SDK approach. Enables MCP servers to gain Nostr interoperability without rewriting. Proxy pattern is key innovation: existing MCP servers maintain conventional transports while gaining decentralized discovery via Nostr. Now production-ready with Bun-based build system and comprehensive documentation. Represents the synthesis of two important protocols (MCP + Nostr).

name: Nostr MCP Servers (lightning-mcp, nostr-mcp)
description: MCP servers enabling AI agents to post to Nostr and make Lightning payments. Abdel Stark's nostr-mcp (now with SSE support, Smithery distribution, and Lightning zaps). Austin Kelsay's nostr-mcp-server with 18+ tool suite. Allows Claude Desktop, GPT, and other MCP-compatible LLMs to interact with Nostr+Lightning directly.
review: Developer tooling proliferating organically. Multiple independent implementations suggest healthy ecosystem growth, not single-vendor push. If MCP becomes standard, these bridges matter. Now production-ready with Smithery one-line install (npx -y @smithery/cli install @AbdelStark/nostr-mcp), SSE transport mode, and send_zap functionality. Community-driven, open source.

name: Amboss
description: Lightning Network analytics and routing optimization platform. Provides data on node capacity, channel health, routing success rates. Used by Lightning operators to optimize their nodes. Useful for researchers tracking network growth and AI agent adoption patterns.
review: Infrastructure analytics layer. Not for agents directly, but critical for understanding Lightning Network health. When we report '5,637 BTC capacity' or 'institutional nodes dominate routing,' this is the data source. Transparency tool for honest research.

name: 1ML Lightning Network Explorer
description: Public Lightning Network statistics tracker. Reports node counts, channel counts, capacity, growth rates, Tor node percentage. Updated continuously. Primary data source for tracking baseline Lightning metrics and measuring AI agent adoption impact over time.
review: Public scoreboard for Lightning Network. This is where we get 'nodes +8.86% monthly' and 'capacity 2,646 BTC' data. When AI agents deploy at scale, we'll see it here first. Essential tool for evidence-based research—transparent, public, continuously updated.

name: Speed (Tether-backed)
description: Tether-backed startup focusing on stablecoin payments over Lightning Network. $8M funding led by Tether (Dec 2025). Aims to bridge stablecoin stability with Lightning speed/cost. Integrates Taproot Assets protocol for USDT on Lightning.
review: Interesting hybrid like Taproot Assets—Lightning infrastructure with stablecoin asset. If this model succeeds, it validates Lightning's technical architecture while remaining agnostic on Bitcoin-as-money. Tether's backing suggests institutional belief in Lightning+stablecoin combination. Worth watching.

name: ln.bot
description: Lightning wallet built specifically for AI agents. Features CLI, MCP server, and API access. One-command wallet creation (lnbot init). L402 middleware for paywalling APIs. Instant settlement between ln.bot wallets (free). Inter-wallet transfers at 0.1% + network fee. No node or channel management required.
review: Zero-ops Lightning wallet designed from scratch for machines, not humans. MCP server enables automatic tool discovery by AI models. L402 middleware allows one-line API monetization. The 'ln.bot→ln.bot: free' pricing creates network effects—agents benefit when other agents use the same service. Cleanest UX for developers who want agent payments without infrastructure overhead.

name: Lightning Wallet MCP
description: MCP server giving AI agents Bitcoin wallets via Lightning Network. v1.1 adds X402 protocol support (USDC on Base) as automatic fallback alongside L402. Features: operator/agent hierarchy with spending limits, webhook notifications, keysend payments, invoice decoding, agent analytics, budget management, agent-to-agent transfers, and account recovery. 2,839+ real Lightning transactions completed in production.
review: Production-ready MCP server with unique dual-protocol support (L402 + X402). Auto-detection means agents can pay any API regardless of whether it uses Lightning or USDC. Operator/agent hierarchy enables enterprise use cases with spending controls. Self-registration option requires no pre-existing account—agents can onboard themselves. True economic autonomy for AI agents. Proven usage with thousands of real transactions.

name: Tether Wallet Toolkit
description: Open-source wallet development kit from Tether supporting both humans and AI agents. Multi-blockchain support including Bitcoin, Lightning Network, Ethereum, Solana, and TON. Provides standardized wallet interfaces for agent integration. Open-sourced October 2025.
review: Tether's entry into AI agent wallet infrastructure signals institutional recognition of the category. Multi-chain support means agents aren't locked to a single ecosystem. Bitcoin+Lightning support included but not exclusive—represents 'agnostic' approach vs Bitcoin-native philosophy. Corporate backing provides resources but raises questions about decentralization. Interesting counterpoint to Lightning Labs' Bitcoin-only approach.

name: Lightning Enable MCP
description: Open-source MCP server enabling AI agents to make autonomous Bitcoin Lightning payments. Available in .NET and Python. Features: pay invoices, access L402 APIs, budget tracking, create invoices, USD/BTC exchange via Strike. Supports Strike API, LND, NWC (CoinOS, CLINK, Alby), and OpenNode. Includes live L402 demo store at store.lightningenable.com. Now with 23 tools including Agent Service Agreement (ASA) tools for agent-to-agent commerce on Nostr.
review: Multi-language MCP server (.NET + Python) with broad wallet support. L402-native design with automatic payment handling for API access. Budget controls and spending limits address runaway spending concerns. Live demo store proves real-world functionality. Docker support simplifies deployment. Strong documentation and 23 available tools including agent-to-agent commerce via Nostr. Production-ready for agent developers.

name: AgentBTC MCP Server
description: BYON (Bring Your Own Node) Bitcoin/Lightning MCP server. Non-custodial, zero-trust architecture—node credentials never leave your machine. 13 MCP tools for wallet management, invoices, payments, channels, and L402. Features Lightning addresses, spending policies (per-transaction/daily/monthly limits), and full audit trail. Works with Claude Desktop, OpenClaw, any MCP client.
review: First-mover advantage claimed as 'world's first Bitcoin/Lightning MCP server.' BYON architecture prioritizes security—credentials stay local, no counterparty risk. 13 tools cover complete Lightning operations including channel management. Spending policies with granular limits solve the 'runaway agent' problem. Lightning addresses (bot@yourname.agentbtc.io) enable human-readable payments. Good fit for security-conscious developers with existing LND nodes.

name: bitcoin-mcp
description: Model Context Protocol (MCP) server enabling AI models to interact with Bitcoin and Lightning Network. Features key generation, address validation, transaction decoding, blockchain queries (latest block, transaction details), and Lightning Network integration. Supports Claude Desktop, Goose, and any MCP-compatible LLM. Published on npm, includes CI/CD and comprehensive documentation.
review: New addition to the growing MCP server ecosystem. Differentiates with combined Bitcoin on-chain and Lightning capabilities—agents can generate keys, validate addresses, decode transactions, AND make Lightning payments. Multi-client support (Claude, Goose) with video demos. Published to npm with proper CI/CD. Another signal that Bitcoin-native agent tooling is proliferating organically beyond single-vendor control.

name: Danke MCP
description: MCP server enabling AI agents to earn and send sats on the Danke network—a gratitude-based Bitcoin Lightning micropayment system. Agents can register with Nostr identity, thank other agents with sats, check balances, withdraw via Lightning invoices, and view leaderboards. Plug-and-play with Claude Desktop, OpenClaw, and any MCP-compatible client. One-command install via npm.
review: Social layer for agent payments—gratitude-based micropayments create positive-sum reputation dynamics. Nostr-based identity means agents maintain consistent cross-platform reputation. Novel approach: agents earn sats by being helpful, not just charging for services. Leaderboard creates visibility for high-value agents. Represents evolution beyond pure commerce toward community-driven value recognition. Could become important reputation primitive in multi-agent ecosystems.

name: btcpay-mcp
description: MCP server for BTCPay Server—the only MCP server exposing full merchant payment processing. 21 tools covering invoice creation, store management, pull payments, payment requests, Lightning operations, wallet management, webhooks, and exchange rates. Supports BTCPay Server Greenfield API. Python-based, published to PyPI. One-line install: pip install btcpay-mcp. Default testnet support for development.
review: First and only MCP server for BTCPay Server merchant infrastructure. Complements blockchain data servers (like bitcoin-mcp) with complete payment processing capabilities. 21 tools enable agents to create invoices, manage stores, handle refunds, and monitor payments. Ideal for agents operating e-commerce or subscription businesses. PyPI distribution and uvx support make deployment trivial. 77 tests pass with mock-based testing. Significant gap filled in the Bitcoin agent tooling ecosystem.

name: aiprox-mcp
description: MCP server for AIProx—autonomous agent registry and multi-rail payment orchestrator. Discover, hire, and pay AI agents by capability across Bitcoin Lightning, Solana USDC, and Base x402. 26 live agents with transparent pricing. Features orchestrator mode (decomposes tasks into subtasks), strict pipeline mode (explicit agent chaining), and WaaS (Workflows as a Service) for scheduled multi-agent pipelines.
review: Unique multi-rail approach supporting Bitcoin Lightning, Solana USDC, and Base x402 simultaneously. 26 live agents with capability-based discovery—no hardcoded integrations required. Orchestrator can decompose complex tasks and route to specialist agents automatically. Strict pipeline mode enables deterministic execution chains. WaaS supports scheduled workflows. Represents practical agnosticism: agents pay with whatever rail the service accepts. Interesting model for agent service marketplaces.
